coredata.dc:title: Phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-silicon nanoparticle composite as an anode material for lithium ion batteries with largely enhanced electrochemical performance
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lijuan, Kong
Yongqiang, Yang
Ruiyi, Li
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Silicon has been recognized as the most promising anode material for high-capacity lithium ion batteries. However, its large volume variation during the charge-discharge process results in electrode pulverization and fast capacity loss on cycling. The paper reports a facile synthesis of phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PF-GQD) through the pyrolysis of citric acid and phenylalanine. The PF-GQD was coated on the surface of silicon nanoparticles (SiNP) and then treated by thermal annealing in Ar/H2 to obtain PF-GQD@SiNP composite. The PF-GQD coating layer not only improves the electrical conductivity, but also effectively prevents the direct contact of silicon surface with the electrolyte molecules. The composite electrode exhibits an excellent electrochemical performance for lithium ion batteries. The specific capacity is 4066mAhg−1 at 50mAg−1, 3796mAhg−1 at 100mAg−1 and 1820mAhg−1 at 1000mAg−1. The capacity can remain 3068mAhg−1 after 100 cycles at 100mAg−1. As a control sample, alanine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (AF-GQD) was also prepared and used for the fabrication of AF-GQD@SiNP composite. The result shows that the electrochemical performance of PF-GQD@SiNP is much better than that of AF-GQD@SiNP. The improvement is attributed to the benzene ring at the edge of graphene sheets. Its introduction creates a wider and finer energy level of electron and well-defined steric structure compared to AF-GQD, which further accelerates the electron transfer and electrolyte transport and leads to an improved electrochemical performance. In addition, the study also provides an economic, eco-friendly and facile method for the fabrication of silicon-based anode materials for next generation high-performance lithium ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon anode
Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical performance
Lithium ion batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115 ). Prospective Joint Research Project: Cooperative Innovation Fund (No. BY2014023-01 and No. BY2015019-26 ) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project ( B13025 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for synthesis of PF-GQD@SiNP.
TEM (a), HRTEM (b), FFTP (c) images and particle size distribution (d) of PF-GQD.
TEM (a), enlarged TEM (b), HRTEM images (c) and EDS patterns (d) of PF-GQD@SiNP.
The IR spectra (A) of the pristine silicon powder (a) and PF-GQD@SiNP (b) and the enlarged IR spectrum (B) of PF-GQD@SiNP between 500cm−1 and 1500cm−1.
Raman spectrum (A) and enlarged IR spectrum between 1000cm−1 and 3000cm−1 of the PF-GQD@SiNP.
First charge/discharge curve of the coin cell using PF-GQD@SiNP (a), AF-GQD@SiNP (b) or SiNP as working electrode (c).
The first charge/discharge curves of the coin cell with PF-GQD@SiNP working electrode at 50mAg−1 (a), 100mAg−1 (b), 200mAg−1 (c), 500mAg−1 (d), 1000mAg−1 (e), 1500mAg−1 (f) and 2000mAg−1 (g).
The specific discharge capacities of the coin cells with different working electrodes at different rates.
The cyclic performance of the coin cell with PF-GQD@SiNP and SiNP working electrodes at 100mAg−1.

A: The AC impedance spectra of the three-electrode cell with PF-GQD@SiNP working electrode after 1- (a), 2- (b) and 3-cycle with the equivalent circuit from the EIS measurements (inset). B: The AC impedance spectra of the three-electrode cell with SiNP (a) and AF-GQD@SiNP working electrodes (b).
The fluorescence emission (a) and excitation spectrum (b) of PF-GQD (A) and AF-GQD (B).
The charge and discharge plateau potential difference of different electrode at various rates.

row.entry: The charge and discharge plateau potential difference (V) at different current density (mAg−1)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impedance parameters calculated from equivalent circuits.

head.ce:title: Phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-silicon nanoparticle composite as an anode material for lithium ion batteries with largely enhanced electrochemical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Graphene Inspection Technology Key Laboratory, Jiangsu Province Special Equiment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch of Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Graphene Inspection Technology Key Laboratory
Jiangsu Province Special Equiment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch of Wuxi

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported a facile synthesis of phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dots via pyrolysis of the mixture of citric acid and phenylalanine and its use for fabrication of phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dots-silicon nanoparticles (termed PF-GQD@SiNP). The introduction of PF-GQD greatly improves the electrical conductivity and effectively prevents direct contact of silicon nanoparticles with the electrolyte. Owing to unique structure of PF-GQD, the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode provides an excellent electrochemical performance for lithium ion batteries.

Silicon has been recognized as the most promising anode material for high-capacity lithium ion batteries. However, its large volume variation during the charge-discharge process results in electrode pulverization and fast capacity loss on cycling. The paper reports a facile synthesis of phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PF-GQD) through the pyrolysis of citric acid and phenylalanine. The PF-GQD was coated on the surface of silicon nanoparticles (SiNP) and then treated by thermal annealing in Ar/H2 to obtain PF-GQD@SiNP composite. The PF-GQD coating layer not only improves the electrical conductivity, but also effectively prevents the direct contact of silicon surface with the electrolyte molecules. The composite electrode exhibits an excellent electrochemical performance for lithium ion batteries. The specific capacity is 4066mAhg−1 at 50mAg−1, 3796mAhg−1 at 100mAg−1 and 1820mAhg−1 at 1000mAg−1. The capacity can remain 3068mAhg−1 after 100 cycles at 100mAg−1. As a control sample, alanine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (AF-GQD) was also prepared and used for the fabrication of AF-GQD@SiNP composite. The result shows that the electrochemical performance of PF-GQD@SiNP is much better than that of AF-GQD@SiNP. The improvement is attributed to the benzene ring at the edge of graphene sheets. Its introduction creates a wider and finer energy level of electron and well-defined steric structure compared to AF-GQD, which further accelerates the electron transfer and electrolyte transport and leads to an improved electrochemical performance. In addition, the study also provides an economic, eco-friendly and facile method for the fabrication of silicon-based anode materials for next generation high-performance lithium ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: The lithium ion batteries (LIBs) with high power density and energy density are in increasingly demand owing to a substantial market growth for renewable energy storage and electric vehicle applications [1]. Unfortunately, the characteristics of currently commercialized graphite such as low theoretical capacity and poor rate-capability are not enough to be a suitable anode material for high-performance LIBs [2]. A considerable effort has been dedicated to develop new carbonaceous materials [3] and high-capacity non-carbonaceous materials in the recent years, including tin- [4], silicon- [5], and metal-oxide-based anode materials [6]. Silicon anode has received the most considerable attention owing to its highest storage lithium capacity (4200mAhg−1) [7]. However, a crucial drawback of silicon is to undergo huge volume change during the charge and discharge process. This results in severe mechanical stress and pulverization of the silicon anode. This, combined with the intrinsically low electrical conductivity of silicon anode, has prevented its practical application.

Many ways have been successfully used for improving the electrochemical properties of silicon anode. One way is to reduce the size of the silicon particles to nanoscale [8], as this can reduce the lithium ion diffusion distance and relieve the stress associated with the volume change during the electrochemical alloying. Cho and Tom Ficraux reported the synthesis of silicon nanowires, in which nanoporous alumina was used as the template [9]. The use of the template eliminates the growth of silicon underlayer island. The resulted silicon nanowire anode exhibits an enhanced cycling stability for LIBs. However, the practical effect of the way is very limited. On the one hand, reduce size of silicon particles does not really decrease the volume change rate, so it is impossible to fundamentally eliminate the problem of the powder of electrode material caused by the volume change of silicon. On the other hand, reduce size will largely increase the specific surface area of silicon. This make more silicon atoms can react with the electrolyte molecules to form more by-products, resulting in a faster capacity fading [10]. Another way is to incorporate metal atoms into silicon crystal to form a silicon-metal alloy [11]. The result shows that the introduction of highly conducting metal not only improves the electrical conductivity, but also reduces the volume change during the charge and discharge processes via the volume compensation between alloy components. This largely decreases the capacity fading caused by the volume change [12]. Song et al. reported a new nitridated Si-Ti-Ni alloy as an anode for LIBs [13]. The investigation demonstrated that the nitride formation leads to an increased capacity of 14% and high rate-performance of 4 times. However, the addition of conducting metal results in the loss of the electrochemical stability of silicon owing to its highly chemical activity. In addition, the silicon-metal alloy always offers a relatively lower storage lithium capacity compared to pristine silicon anode, because theoretical storage lithium capacity of the alloying element mostly is much lower than that of silicon crystal. Recently, the hybridization of silicon with carbon material to construct the silicon-carbon composite become one research hot [14]. A surface coating structure in which silicon is coated with amorphous carbon [15], or a core-shell structure whereby nano-silicon is encapsulated within hollow spheres [16]. Regardless of the specific structure, the additional conductive component serves to improve the electrical conductivity, while also restricting or accommodating the large silicon volume change. In this regard, the silicon/carbon composite presents an effective solution owing to the inherently high electrical conductivity of carbon. As a new type of carbon materials, graphene has becomingly received great concern for the silicon modification [17]. Graphene is two dimensional macromolecular carbon material with remarkable electrical conductivity, large specific surface and good mechanical property. These characteristics make it become an ideal conductive additive and structural support for silicon crystal [18]. However, classical graphene has a relatively big size of graphene sheets from several microns to tens of microns. Coat such large sheets on the surface of silicon nanoparticles seriously hinders the entry of lithium ions into silicon crystal, leading to a low capacity for LIBs. To resolve the problem, two-dimensional graphene sheets must be converted into the three-dimensional graphene with a special space structure [19] or activated graphene with a rich of porous structures [20]. However, the production of three-dimensional graphene or activated graphene often requires a complex and time-consuming process. Moreover, the use of large amounts of graphene also increases the cost, which are not in favor of the commercial process. Unlike classical graphene, graphene quantum dots (GQD) are composed of single- or few-layer graphene sheets with a tiny size of only several nanometers. If the use of GQD instead of classical graphene for the silicon modification, the effect of the coating layer on the lithium ion diffusion between the silicon surface and the electrolyte will be reduced to a very low level due to their very small size. In the previous work, our group coated GQD on the surface of LTO nanoparticles to obtain the LTO-GQD nanohybrid. The nanohybrid electrode displays an obviously enhanced electrochemical performance for LIBs [21]. To the best of our knowledge, however, no report refers to the applications of GQD in the silicon anode up to now.

Herein, we reported a facile synthesis of two kinds of amino acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots through the pyrolysis of the mixture of citric acid and phenylalanine or alanine, which are assigned as PF-GQD and AF-GQD, and their use for the fabrication of graphene quantum dot-silicon nanoparticle composite (termed GQD@SiNP). The study demonstrates that the introduction of GQD improves the electrical conductivity and effectively prevents direct contact of silicon surface with the electrolyte molecules. The PF-GQD@SiNP electrode provides the advantage of electrochemical performance for LIBs compared to the AF-GQD@SiNP anode and the SiNP anode.

The PF-GQD was made through the pyrolysis of the mixture of citric acid and phenylalanine [22]. In a typical synthesis, citric acid (100mmol) and phenylalanine (90mmol) were dissolved in ultra pure water (50ml). The mixed solution was evaporated at 80°C until dry. After that, the resulting slurry was transferred into a 100ml of autoclave pressure vessel. Flowed by heating at 180°C for 3h and cooling to room temperature. The resulting PF-GQD sample was dissolved in ultra pure water to form a transparent PF-GQD solution (20mgmL−1). The solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a 3kDa cut-off molecular weight with change of water every 6h before the precipitate occurred in the bag. After that, the solution in the bag was dried by free-drying to obtain a solid PF-GQD product. The above procedure is also used for the preparation of AF-GQD only using alanine instead of phenylalanine.

The silicon powder (400mg, 99% purity, average particle size of 100nm, from Hefei Kaier Nanometer Energy Polytron Technologies Inc.) was dispersed in the mixed solution of acetone, ethanol and water with the volume ratio of 1:1:1. Followed by the treatment with ultrasonic for 40minutes to and centrifugal separation. The collected silicon powder was immersed in the HF aqueous solution (4%) for 2minutes. After washed with ultra pure water for two times, the silicon powder was dispersed in the mixed solution of ammonia (30%), hydrogen peroxide (30%) and water with the volume ratio of 1:1:5. Flowed by heating at 100°C for 30minutes and washing with ultra pure water for three times to obtain hydrophilic silicon powder.

The hydrophilic silicon powder and 20wt% poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (termed PDDA) (6g, Sigma Aldrich, MW=10000-20000) aqueous solution were dispersed in ultra pure water (200mL) under the sonication. Excess PDDA was washed away with ultra pure water by the centrifugation (10000rpm) four times. Followed by drying at 65°C in vacuum. The obtained positively charged silicon powder was dispersed in ultra pure water (50mL). Then, dropwise added the PF-GQD aqueous solution (20ml, 5mgml−1) in the above dispersion under stirring and followed by the centrifugation (4000rpm) for 10minutes. To prepare PF-GQD@SiNP composite, the collected solid was dried at 60°C in a vacuum and then treated by thermal annealing in Ar/H2 (95:5v/v) at 400°C for 1h. In addition, the above procedure is also used for the preparation of AF-GQD@SiNP composite only using AF-GQD instead of PF-GQD.

Thermal gravimetric analysis (in air, 750°C) was used to determine the content of the GQD in the composite. The result shows that the content of GQD is 8.45% for the PF-GQD@SiNP and 8.17% for the AF-GQD@SiNP.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) analysis were performed in a HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. SEM samples were done by slowly dropping diluted ethanol dispersion of the products on a copper sheet attached to an aluminum sample holder, and the solvent need to be completely evaporated at room temperature. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was carried out on a JEOL 2010 (HR) transmission electron microscope at 200 keV. The samples were prepared by dispersing a small amount of powder-like products into absolute ethanol. Then, one drop of the above suspension was dropped on the surface of 300mesh copper and was dried under a infra-red lamp for volatilizing ethanol. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a X-ray D8 Advance instrument operated at 40kV and 20mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ=0.15406nm. Infrared spectra (IR) were recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer and Raman measurements were carried out using InVia laser micro-Raman spectrometer. Fluorescence spectrum and intensity were recorded on a Cary Esclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan). Fluorescence intensity decay curves were measured on QM/TM system (Photon Technology International). AFM measurements were performed using a Quesant AFM (Aguora Hills, CA) operating in tapping mode in air on room temperature, with standard silicone tips (NanoAndMore Gmbh, Wetzlar, Germany) and with the constant force of 40N/m.

Electrochemical properties of LTO materials were evaluated using 2016 coin cells and three-electrode cells. The active material (PF-GQD@SiNP, AF-GQD@SiNP or SiNP), acetylene black and sodium alginate (2%) was mixed based on a mass ratio of 70: 15: 15 under vigorous ultrasonication to form a homogeneous slurry, which were then coated on copper foils. The loading density of active materials was about 3.0mgcm−2. Subsequently dried in a vacuum oven at 120°C overnight and rolled by using a rolling machine. The copper foil was placed on the specialized slicing machine to prepare the working electrode. The working electrodes was incorporated into a 2016 coin cell, in which Li foils were serviced as the counter and reference electrode, Celgard 2400 as the separator, and a mixed solvent of ethylene carbonate, dimethyl carbonate and diethylene carbonate (1:1:1) containing of 1M LiPF6 as the electrolyte. The above working electrode, counter electrode, separator and electrolyte were also used to made a three-electrode cell (shown in Fig. 1 ). The three-electrode cell used a Li foil as the reference electrode that the diameter of the reference electrode is about 1/3 of the working electrode, which is set outside the working electrode and counter electrode. The assembly process of both the coin cell and the three-electrode cell was conducted in an argon-filled glove box having O2 and H2O contents below 0.1ppm.

The cells were cycled in the potential range of 0.001∼2.5V (vs. Li/Li+) with specific currents between 50mAg−1 and 2000mAg−1 using a CT2001A LAND Battery Test System. All cells were kept at open circuit potential for 24h before starting galvanostatic cyclations, in order to allow an appropriate wetting of the electrodes. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out on the three-electrode cell using a CHI 660D Electrochemical Workstation. A potential amplitude of ±5mV and a frequency range of 0.01 to 105Hz were adopted. For the sake of comparison, all specific capacity, current and resistance values are normalized to overall active material mass.

The synthesis of PF-GQD@SiNP includes four assemble processes (shown in Fig. 2 ). The first process is to convert hydrophobic silicon into hydrophilic silicon. Firstly, silicon powder was dispersed in the mixed solution of acetone, ethanol and water. Followed by the ultrasonic treatment to strip away the greasy dirt on the silicon surface. Then, the silicon powder was immersed in the HF solution to remove the silicon dioxide on the surface of silicon particles. Finally, the treated silicon powder was partly oxidized by hydrogen peroxide in the ammonia medium. The oxidization leads to the formation of large hydrophilic groups on the surface of silicon spheres such as hydroxyl groups, improving the water-solubility of silicon. Hence, the resulted hydrophilic silicon powder can be well dispersed in water to form a stable dispersion (see Fig. s1). The second process is to modify the silicon surface with PDDA to form positively charged silicon powder. PDDA is a kind of commonly used cationic polyelectrolyte, the excellent water-solubility make it is easy to well contact with the hydrophilic silicon to form the composite by their electrostatic attraction. As it is added, the zeta potential of the silicon dispersion is gradually changed from −25mV into +37mV, indicating the formation of negatively charged silicon nanoparticles. The third process is to coat the PF-GQD on the surface of the silicon nanoparticles modified with PDDA. In the step, the negatively charged PF-GQD was mixed with the positively charged silicon powder under stirring to rapidly form PF-GQD@PDDA/SiNP composite due to their electrostatic attraction. The fourth process is to reduce the composite. Our investigation confirms that the unreduced PF-GQD is not suitable to be used as the electrode materials for LIBs. On the one hand, the poor electrical conductivity of the unreduced PF-GQD brings a low high-rate capability for LIBs. The addition of large conductive agent was strongly required to improve the electrical conductivity of the composite electrode. This will increase the mass and volume of the electrode, which limits its application in high-powder batteries. On the other hand, the PF-GQD contains a rich of active groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. These active groups can combine with lithium ions to produce irreversible capacity. In addition, the free water produced by the decomposition of active groups in the PF-GQD and SiNP causes a decline in the electrochemical performance [23,24]. The study verifies that the thermal annealing plays important roles in improving the electrochemical performance of the composite electrode. During the thermal annealing, most of the active groups attached on the graphene sheets and the PDDA adsorbed on the silicon surface will be decomposed. This leads to the enhanced electrical conductivity and the reduced irreversible capacity. In addition, the partially reduced graphene sheets were coalesced into a cross-links of three-dimensional graphene network with the assistance of van der Waals' forces and p-p stacking. This not only further improves the electron transfer, but also accelerates the electrolyte transport during the electrode reaction. However, the PF-GQD may come off from the silicon surface if all active groups were removed from the composite. The result proves that the apparent loss of PF-GQD can be observed if the temperature excess 500°C. For the reason, the thermal temperature of 400°C was selected for the reduction of the composite.

Fig. 3 presents TEM image, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) patterns, fast Fourier transform pattern (FFTP) and particle size distribution histogram of PF-GQD. According to the analysis of TEM image, the PF-GQD exhibits approximately two-dimensional sheet shape with a low degree of the agglomeration. The particle size distribution histogram is obtained using an image processing software. The size of graphene sheets in the PF-GQD is distributed in narrow range of 1–7nm with the average particle size of 3.1nm. The result of FFTP and HRTEM analysis prove that the PF-GQD offers a crystalline hexagonal structure. In addition, atomic force microscopic (AFM) image of the PF-GQD in Fig. s2 reveals that their heights are less than 1.5nm, suggesting that there are one to three layers of graphene oxide-like structures [25].

TEM image, HRTEM pattern and energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) of the PF-GQD@SiNP composite are shown in Fig. 4 . The TEM analysis shows the encapsulation of silicon particles with one layer of PF-GQD. The enlarged TEM further verifies the existence of PF-GQD on the surface of silicon sphere. The PF-GQD layer can effectively prevent the direct contact of silicon surface with the electrolyte molecules, leading to an improved cycling stability during the charge and discharge process. However, we also observe from Fig. 3b that the part of silicon surface is exposed to the outside, which is beneficial to the lithium ion diffusion between the silicon surface and the electrolyte. The result of HRTEM analysis reveals the crystallinity of PF-GQD@SiNP. The lattice spacings of 0.22nm and 0.32nm are agreed with that of the XRD analysis. The former is corresponded to the crystal plane (110) of PF-GQD. The latter is corresponded to the crystal plane (111) of silicon crystal. The selected region EDS analysis proves that the composite is composed of silicon (Si), carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) elements. Here, Si element comes from the silicon particles, and C and N elements come from the PF-GQD. The existence of O element confirms that some oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl groups still remain on the silicon surface and PF-GQD after thermal annealing, which has been demonstrated by the result of IR analysis.

The IR spectra of silicon powder and PF-GQD@SiNP are shown in Fig. 5 . There are several weak IR absorption peaks on the IR spectrum of silicon powder. The absorption band at around 1092cm−1 is the anti symmetric stretching vibration absorption of Si-O-Si. The peak at 798cm−1 is the symmetric stretching vibration absorption of Si-O bond. The absorption band at around 3450cm−1 is the symmetric stretching vibration absorption of the OH in water. The peak at 1638cm−1 is the bending vibration absorption of the H-O-H in water. The peak at 955cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibration absorption of Si-O-H. The above results demonstrate the existence of small amount of SiO2·H2O on the silicon surface. As these peaks are very weak, the IR absorption of PF-GQD@SiNP is mainly attributed to the PF-GQD. The IR spectrum of the composite indicates a CO stretch vibration band at 1701cm−1 as well as a possible C-H stretching vibration at about 1380cm−1. This suggested the presence of carbonyl and C-H groups. Bands present at 1566cm−1 and 3450cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of COO- and OH groups. Besides the above peaks, we also observe a great number of absorption peaks between 500cm−1 and 1500cm−1 (shown in Fig. 5B). After the comparison with the IR spectra of citric acid and phenylalanine, we find that these peaks includes the characteristics IR absorption peaks of main functional groups in the citric acid and phenyalanine. The result demonstrates that the functional groups in citric acid and phenylalanine remained unchanged during the pyrolysis.

The crystal structure of PF-GQD@SiNP was confirmed by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 6 , the composite displays five diffraction peaks at 28.6°, 47.4°, 56.3°, 69.2° and 76.3°, which can be ascribed to the (111), (220), (311), (400) and (331) planes of the silicon crystal [26], respectively. These diffraction peaks are considerably sharp, suggesting highly crystalline nature of silicon particles. The crystalline feature will help to increase the electrical conductivity, leading to a high electrochemical performance for LIBs. After coated PF-GQD, the above diffraction peaks still remain unchanged in the terms of position and but decrease in intensity. In the study, the peak at (111) is taken to evaluate the crystallite size of PF-GQD@SiNP and pristine SiNP. The crystallite sizes are calculated via the Scherrer’s equation:
(1)
d =
k λ
β cos θ
where d is the crystallite size, λ is the wavelength of X-ray, k is 0.89 as the shape factor, θ is the diffraction angle of the peak, and β is the true half-peak width. According to the calculated results, the PF-GQD@SiNP and SiNP possess the crystalline size of 48.33nm and 48.41nm, respectively. The result also verifies that the introduction of PF-GQD don’t effect on the crystal structure of silicon. Often, graphene offer a weak and wide diffraction peak at 26°. Because the PF-GQD has a lower degree of crystallization compared to the classical graphene, leading to a decreased peak intensity. In addition, the existence of silicon further decreases the crystallization degree of PF-GQD, resulting in a reduce intensity of the diffraction peak at 26°. Therefore, the diffraction peak at 26° is so weak that we can not observe it on the XRD patterns of PF-GQD@SiNP.

Raman spectroscopy was often used to investigate the surface structure of the composite. Fig. 7 indicates that the Raman spectroscopy of PF-GQD@SiNP has a strong peak at about 500cm−1 and several weak peaks. The peak at around 500cm−1 and two weaker peaks at about 292cm−1 and 915cm−1 can be attributed to the elemental silicon. For the silicon crystal, no any Raman peaks appear in the range of 1000–2500cm−1. Therefore, the Raman spectroscopy is attributed to PF-GQD. Raman spectrum of carbon materials is characterized by two main features: G band arising from the first order scattering of the E 1g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms, and D band arising from a breathing mode of point photons of A1g symmetry [27]. Fig. 7B shows that two typical Raman peaks of PF-GQD, including D band (1385cm−1) and G band (1575cm−1). The fact verifies the existences of PF-GQD in the PF-GQD@SiNP again. Besides, a relative intensity of the “disorder” D-band and the crystalline G-band (ID/IG) value was currently used for evaluating the dispersibility of PF-GQD. With decrease of the crystallization degree of carbon materials, the peak intensity of D band increases and the intensity of G band decreases. Based on the data in Fig. 7B, we can calculate the ID/IG value of the PF-GQD in the composite. The ID/IG is 1.41, indicating that the graphene sheets in the PF-GQD have been fully dispersed in the composite.

To demonstrate potential application of the GQD@SiNP prepared by using inexpensive GQD as the coating material, we conducted a series of investigations to determine the electrochemical performance for LIBs. Herein, the GQD@SiNP or SiNP as the active material and acetylene black as the conducting agent were dispersed in the sodium alginate solution to form a homogeneous slurry, and then deposited on a copper current collector. The resulted anodes were assembled into coin cells with a lithium counter electrode and underwent the galvanostatic charge/discharge cycling. The first charge/discharge curves of PF-GQD@SiNP cell, AF-GQD@SiNP cell and SiNP coin cell in the potential range of 0.001–2.5V at the current of 100mAg−1 are presented in Fig. 8 . For the each cell, its first discharge capacity is less than the corresponding charge capacity. The enhanced discharge capacity is mainly attributed to the formation of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) [27]. For the SiNP cell, the charge capacity and discharge capacity are 2759.6mAhg−1 and 2101.2mAhg−1. The columbic efficiency is less than 76%. Such a low columbic efficiency is because the silicon surface in the SiNP electrode can fully contact with the electrolyte to form the SEI film and leads to an increased discharge capacity with a low columbic efficiency. In contrast to the SiNP cell, the AF-GQD@SiNP cell displays a much higher columbic efficiency of 92.5% with a similar discharge capacity of 2669.1mAhg−1. Furthermore, the columbic efficiency will increase to 99.5% if AF-GQD was replaced by PF-GQ. The result demonstrates that the GQDs coating layer can effectively prevent direct contact of the silicon surface with the electrolyte molecules. This largely reduces the number of the silicon atoms that can react with the electrolyte to form the SEI film, indicating an improved columbic efficiency. As the PF-GQD and AF-GQD have a similar chemical composition and structure. Their main difference is the existence of the benzene rings on the edge of graphene sheets in the PF-GQD. Therefore, the above result also proves that the introduction of benzene ring into the GQD can further improve the electrochemical performance of the composite electrode.

The charge and discharge curves of PF-GQD@SiNP coin cell at different rates from 50mAg−1 to 2000mAg−1 are presented in Fig. 9 . The discharge capacity of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode reaches 4066mAhg−1 at 50mAg−1, which is obviously higher than that of AF-GQD@SiNP electrode (2989.6mAhg−1) and SiNP electrode (3212.7mAhg−1). At all rates the discharge capacity of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode is higher than that of other electrodes with a higher columbic efficiency, which the difference increases with the increase of the rate. This is attributed to the introduction of PF-QDD. On the one hand, the PF-GQD layer effectively prevents the direct contact between the silicon surface and the electrolyte, resulting in a reduced loss of the electrode active substance caused by the formation of SEI film. On the other hand, the excellent electrical conductivity of PF-GQD@SiNP greatly improves the electrochemical performance. Often, at low current density almost all active surface area of the electrode can well contact with lithium ions to react completely, leading to a higher capacity. The effective interaction between lithium ions and the electrode material is largely reduced at high current density, leading to a lower capacity. Since the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode provides an ultra fast electron transfer and the electrolyte transport during the charge and discharge processes, leading to a greatly improved rate-capacitive behavior.

The SiNP electrode offers a flat operation potential plateau when the charged/discharge is at low rates such as 50mAg−1 and 100mAg−1. The potential plateau of SiNP electrode becomes shorter and gradually bends down with the rate increased, while that of the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode still remains straight line shape. This is because the polarization of SiNP electrode would be increased at an increased rate. A comparison of potential difference (ΔE) between the charge and discharge plateau potentials was taken for the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode, AF-GQD@SiNP electrode and SiNP electrode. The results were listed in Table 1 . The ΔE value represents the degree of polarization of the electrode. A bigger ΔE value means a bigger polarization and poor reaction kinetics, while a small ΔE value means a lower polarization and better reaction kinetics. From Table 1, we find that the ΔE for the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode is far less than that of the SiNP electrode, and slightly less than that of the AF-GQD@SiNP electrode at all discharge rates from 50mAg−1 to 2000mAg−1, indicating a lower polarization and better reaction kinetic. The improvement is mainly be attributed to the introduction of PF-GQD. As a new type of quantum dots, the PF-GQD offers unique property associated with both graphene and quantum dots. This will greatly enhance the electrical conductivity and electrolyte transport, leading to an improved polarization of the electrode. In addition, the result also shows that the introduction of benzene rings into the graphene sheets leads to a smaller polarization of the electrode.

Fig. 10 shows the discharge specific capacity with the number of cycles for the PF-GQD@SiNP cell, AF-GQD@SiNP coin cell and SiNP coin cell at different rates from 50mAg−1 to 2000mAg−1. Three electrodes display different electrochemical performances for LIBs. Compared to the SiNP electrode, the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode exhibits a higher specific capacity and better high-rate capability. The capacity difference between the SiNP and PF-GQD@SiNP can be clearly seen and becomes larger at larger rates. For instance, at a 1000mAg−1, the discharge specific capacity of PF-GQD@SiNP is 1812mAhg−1, which is about 10.8-fold that of SiNP electrode. While at 2000mAg−1, the discharge specific capacity of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode is 1111mAhg−1, which is close to 185-fold that of SiNP electrode. The improved high-rate capability of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode can be explained by the reduced resistance and polarization of the electrode. With the PF-GQD conductive graphene sheets throughout the whole composite material surface, the rich of pathways for the electron transfer are produced, thus the electron transfer is more effective and electrical conductivity of the electrode is improved. In addition, the specific capacity of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode is well restored after the low current rate of 100mAg−1 is used again, indicating good cycle performance and electrochemical stability. However, the specific capacities of AF-GQD@SiNP and SiNP electrodes can be partly restored. This is mainly attributed to the loss of electrode active substance during the charge and discharge processes. In addition, Fig. 9 also indicates that both the specific discharge capacity and high-rate performance of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode are obviously better than that of AF-GQD@SiNP electrode. This is mainly attributed to the difference in the edge structure of graphene sheets between PF-GQD and AF-GQD. On the one hand, the benzene ring in the PF-GQD improves the electrical conductivity of the composite, leading to an enhanced specific capacity and high rate performance. On the other hand, the introduction of benzene rings made a significant steric congestion that interferes with the self-aggregation through p–p stacking interactions. Bending, twisting, and folding of the GQD surface are most likely prevented, implying that effective aromatic conjugation can be extended as a result of steric effects [28]. The steric congestion creates a rich of channels for the electrolyte transport, which further improves the electrochemical performance. For the above reasons, the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode offers largely enhanced capacity and high-rate performance.

The cyclic performance of PF-GQD@SiNP and SiNP cells at the current density of 100mAg−1 is presented in Fig. 11 . A drastic decrease in the capacity of SiNP electrode was observed from the beginning and ultimately becomes zero capacity after 10 cycles. This is because the silicon atoms on the silicon surface directly contact with the electrolyte to form the SEI film. The loss of the electrode active substance rapidly increases with the increase of cycling number, leading to a rapid capacity fading. The cyclic capacity of SiNP electrode continuously decrease and retains only 4.6% at 100th cycle from the second cycle, while the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode shows a capacity retention of 76% under the same condition. At the 100th cycle, its capacity can remain 3068.08mAhg−1, which is at least 8 times higher than the theoretical capacity of commercial graphite (372mAhg−1). More importantly, the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode shows a stable discharge capacity after initial 10 cycles similar to the commercial graphite electrodes. The excellent cycling performance of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode can be attributed to the introduction of PF-GQD. Because the PF-GQD in the PF-GQD@SiNP effectively prevents the direct contact of the silicon surface with the electrolyte molecules, loss of the electrode active substance caused by the formation of SEI film was greatly decreased.

At the same time of measuring the cycle performance of SiNP cell and PF-GQD@SiNP cell, their columbic efficiencies were also investigated for the each of cell cycling and the result was shown in Fig. s3. For the PF-GQD@SiNP cell, the columbic efficiency is about 92% for the first cycle. The value increases to 98% after the first three cycles and stabilizes at about 98% in subsequent cycles, indicating the formation of a stable SEI film on the silicon surface during the first several cycles. The SEI film can greatly reduce the loss of active silicon atoms for the each cycle. Continue to increase the number of cycles, the number of active silicon atoms participating in the electrode reaction is reduced no longer. For the reason, the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode shows a high reversible capacity and good cycling stability with high columbic efficiency for LIBs. However, the columbic efficiency of SiNP cell largely varies with the increase of cell cycles. In fact, the variation reflects a large consumption of active silicon atoms and reduce of reversible capacity for the cell cycling. Thus, the SiNP electrode exhibits a rapid capacity fading with the increasing number of cell cycles. In addition, the above results also demonstrate that the introduction of PF-GQD will lead to an enhanced columbic efficiency and cyclic performance. In the work, the mechanical stability of PF-GQD@SiNP composite was further researched by SEM imaging for electrodes that was removed from a cell after 100 cycles. Cracks and aggregations on the surface of the SiNP electrode are observed in Fig. s4a, which will cause the disconnection of the active materials to the current collector and result in capacity fading. However, No disassembly or cracking of the PF-GQD@SiNP composite was found (Fig. s4b), which indicates that the silicon was still uniformly anchored on the electrode after cycling.

Good columbic efficiency and mechanical stability of the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode are attributed to the introduction of PF-GQD and the use of sodium alginate as the binder for the anode preparation. Similar to conventional graphene oxide, graphene sheets in the PF-GQD will interconnect each other into graphene aerogel on the surface of silicon nanoparticles during the fabrication of PF-GQD@SiNP. Compared with the porous structure constructed by physically and randomly stacked graphene sheets, graphene aerogel possesses much higher electrical conductivity since the constituent graphene sheets are chemically bonded, facilitating much faster charge transport across graphene sheet junctions, and well-defined micro-porous structures, providing fast mass transfer of the electrolyte [29]. These will greatly improve the electrochemical performance of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode for lithium ion batteries. However, graphene sheets in the PF-GQD have much smaller size (about 3nm) and richer hydrophilic groups compared with conventional graphene oxide (from a few microns to tens of microns). The former determines that the formed graphene aerogel is of a very dense graphene network structure. The characteristic make the PF-GQD coating layer can effectively prevent the direct contact between the silicon surface and the electrolyte, greatly reducing the loss of the electrode active material caused by the formation of SEI film and leads to an improved columbic efficiency and cycling stability. The latter determines the graphene aerogel gives an excellent mechanical property due to the existence of great number of graphene sheet junctions. The characteristic make the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode has high cycling stability. In the study, sodium alginate also plays important roles in improving the electrochemical performance of the silicon anodes. As a new binder of silicon anode, sodium alginate posses several critical properties, including little-to-no interaction between the binder and the electrolyte, high ionic conductance via hopping of lithium ions between the adjacent carboxylic cites, and maintaining an almost unchanged surface area [30]. The investigation demonstrates that the use of sodium alginate binder contributes to building on stable and highly conductive SEI film for the first several cycles, leading to an enhanced columbic efficiency and mechanical stability. In addition, the rich of active groups on the edge of graphene sheets in the PF-GQD interact with sodium alginate to further enhance the cycling stability of silicon anode. Therefore, the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode provides a better electrochemical performance compared to the SiNP electrode.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was used to study on the kinetic process of the electrode reaction. Because two-electrode impedance studies have an obvious disadvantage in that it is difficult to distinguish the impedance of anode and cathode from a combined spectra. To measure accurately how a single electrode changes with cycling tests, three-electrode impedance studies are necessary [31]. Recently, Hoshi et al. also reported the optimization of reference electrode position in a three-electrode cell for impedance measurements in LIBs by finite element method [32]. The investigation confirms that outside the area between the working electrode and counter electrode in the symmetrical cell of the LIBs, where the potential gradient and frequency dependence of the potential are very small, is suitable for the placement of the reference electrode. Hence, a model of three-electrode cell was fabricated for the EIS measurement of various electrodes, in which the reference electrode is set outside the working electrode and counter electrode (shown in Fig. 1). Fig. 12 indicates that Nyquist spectra of three anodes compose of one semicircle in high frequency and a subsequent inclined line at the low frequency end. The semicircle in high frequency region is related to the ionic conduction through the electrolyte and electrical conduction between the substrate and the active material. With decreasing the frequency, the spectrum is dominated by the formation of SEI film. Meanwhile, the sloping straight line corresponds to the lithium ion diffusion in the bulk materials [33]. Such a pattern of the EIS can be fitted by the equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 12A. In the equivalent circuit, Rs is the bulk electrolyte resistance, Rct is the electron and charge transfer resistance, Ci is the interfacial capacitance and, Zw is Warburg resistance related to a combination of the diffusional effects of lithium ion on the interface between the active material particles and electrolyte, which is generally indicated by a straight sloping line at low frequency end. In general, the combination of Rct and Zw is called faradic impedance, which reflects kinetics of the cell reaction, and low Rct corresponds to a fast kinetics of the faradic reaction. As shown in Table 2 , the Rct of PF-GQD@SiNP electrode after 1-cycle is much lower than that of SiNP electrode and AF-GQD@SiNP electrode, indicating a faster cell reaction. The result verifies that the introduction of GQD speeds up the faradic reaction of the electrode. Interestingly, the Rct will further reduce with the increase of the cycling. This is attributed to the huge volume change of the silicon nanoparticles during the cycling charge and discharge process. The change of the volume make the PF-GQD is easy to enter into the interior of silicon nanoparticles and enhances the electrical conductivity of the silicon anode, leading to a accelerated cell reaction. Moreover, Table 2 also indicates that the introduction of the benzene ring at the edge of graphene sheets achieves to an obvious increase in the cell reaction rate.

To understand the result of EIS analysis, we studied on the optical features of PF-GQD and AF-GQD. Fig. 13 presents the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PF-GQD and AF-GQD. Often, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra are reflected the characteristics of the material structure and the energy level of the electron, respectively. From Fig. 13, we observe an obvious difference between two excitation spectra. This confirms that the structure in the edge and graphene sheets of PF-GQD is different from that of AF-GQD. In contrast with the AF-GQD, the PF-GQD displays a wider fluorescence spectrum with a higher intensity. As a rule, the fluorescence emission of benzene group in amino acid lie at the wavelength between 300nm and 400nm. Therefore, we suggest that the fluorescence emission of PF-GQD is a proper combination of the fluorescence emissions of the benzene ring and the graphene sheets. More importantly, a wide and fine energy level of PF-GQD may be very close to the energy level of silicon crystal. The coupling of different energy levels of the electron helps to achieve the fast electron and charge transfers between the PF-GQD and the silicon crystal, leading to a fast cell reaction and cell performance.

Based on the electrochemical impedance results, the diffusion coefficients of lithium ions (D Li+ ) in the each cell were calculated by the Equation (2) [34]:
(2)
D
L i
+
R 2
T 2
2
A 2
n 4
F 4
C 2
σ 2
where R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, n is the number of electrons transferred, A is the surface area of the electrode, F is the faraday constant, and C is the concentration of reactants. The value of parameter s is the slope of real part of impedance (Zre) versus inverse square root of radial frequency (ω−1/2) in the Warburg region. The diffusion coefficients on the interface between the active material particles and the electrolyte are calculated to be 1.83×10−13 for the SiNP electrode, 3.41×10−12 for the AF-GQD@SiNP electrode, and 4.52×10−10 for the PF-GQD@SiNP electrode. These data indicate that the introduction of GQD greatly increases the diffusion coefficients of lithium ion. In the study, sodium alginate was used as the binder for the preparation of the anode. The diffusion coefficient depends on the lithium ion transport through the alginate film. The proposed mechanism of ion transport through the alginate film is via hopping of lithium ions between the adjacent carboxylic cites, similarly to alginate’s function for the ion transport in the algae cell [35]. For the AF-GQD@SiNP and PF-GQD@SiNP electrodes, there is one GQD coating layer on the silicon surface. Such one GQD layer offers a more active groups than the sodium alginate film. Thus, the GQD layer is easier to combine with lithium ions to realize a faster lithium ion transport during the electrode reaction. On the other hand, the spongy GQD layer works as an ion reservoir to contain a host of the electrolyte, shortening the transport path of lithium ions. The above two factors result in an increased diffusion coefficient of lithium ion. In addition, the GQD layer also improved the conduction of the electron and simultaneously acted as the mechanical buffer for the expansion. These positive effects of the GQD give rise to low charge transfer resistance, high rate-capability and good cyclic stability. The result listed in Table 2 also shows that the PF-GQD offers a quite advantageous structure for the lithium ion diffusion compared to the AF-GQD, owing to the easy access of lithium ions in the steric structure at the edge of graphene sheets. The easy lithium ion transport into the inverse the steric structure decreases the resistance and increases the rate performance.

We have demonstrated a facile synthesis of two graphene quantum dots through pyrolysis of the mixture of citric acid and phenylalanine or alanine and their use as a coating material for the fabrication of graphene quantum dot-silicon nanoparticle composite. The study reveals that the introduction of graphene quantum dots largely accelerates the electron transfer and the electrolyte transport of the electrode, leading to a better electrochemical performance for lithium ion batteries. Owing to the existence of steric aromatic structure at the edge of graphene sheets, the phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dots as a coating material for the silicon anode futher improve the electrical conductivity and the diffusion of lithium ions between the silicon surface and the electrolyte. The phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-silicon nanoparticle anode exhibits an excellent electrochemical performance for lithium ion batteries and can be widely used as the anode material for high-performance lithium ion batteries. The study also provides an economic, eco-friendly and facile method for the design and preparation of silicon-based anode materials for next generation high performance lithium ion batteries.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115). Prospective Joint Research Project: Cooperative Innovation Fund (No. BY2014023-01 and No. BY2015019-26) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025).
